# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. What are some good movies where people play parodies of themselves?

**Score:** 81
**Body:** I’m talking about Nicholas Cage in unbearable weight of massive talent, but I’d also accept Bob Barker in happy Gilmore, Mike Tyson and the hangover movies, Alice Cooper in Wayne’s World, and Zombieland (don’t ruin the surprise - he’s not listed on the poster). What are your favorites in this category?

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Everyone acting in the movie THIS IS THE END

this is literally what you’re looking for  lol 
  - When Michael Cera slaps Rihanna, I lost it.
    - He has talked about that slap. He asked Rihanna if he could do it and her response was yes if she could slap him back. 

It took multiple times to get the shot right and he said that the last slap caused him to go lay down for half an hour. He said it was worth it and his pain is immortalized in film.
2. Neil Patrick Harris in the Harold and Kumar movies 
  - That role reinvented and revitalized his career
3. The Interview - Eminem
4. I love in Space Balls when John Hurt has the alien burst from his chest and he says “no, not again”.
5. Being John Malkovich 
6. Carl Weather in Arrested Development.

I know it's not a movie, don't @ me 😝
7. Not a movie but the show Extras was pretty great for exactly this kind of thing.

## 2. Movies with unpredictable deaths..which take you by surprise

**Score:** 43
**Body:** I watched The Departed recently and the deaths in this movie happened when you least expected them. And that always hits hard whether it's a movie or a TV show like Game of Thrones. 

So i'm looking to watch these kinds of films. Please drop suggestions, any language works.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Deep Blue Sea
2. Maybe Hereditary? 
3. Sixth Sense
4. Few genres that hit a few different ways: 

* Children of Men (2006)
* L.A. Confidential (1997)
* Burn After Reading (2008) 
* The Darjeeling Limited (2007) 
* Place Beyond the Pines (2012)
  - Burn After Reading surprised me in so many ways. Always love some Coen brothers
5. To Live and Die in LA is famous for this
6. lol Executive Decision 
7. The Mist
8. Burn After Reading 
9. Executive Decision. Like wow didn’t see that coming 

## 3. need movies with amazing soundtracks

**Score:** 32
**Body:** sometimes the music makes the movie even better. i’m looking for films where the soundtrack is a big part of the experience. what are some movies with soundtracks that stick with you long after watching?

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Trainspotting
2. Drive
3. Flash Gordon 
4. Romeo + Juliet (1996)
5. Thelma and Louise! 
Dirty Dancing!
Pulp Fiction!
6. Lost Highway
7. Almost Famous, y'all. The soundtrack is a character in the movie.
8. Baby Driver 100%. Nothing else like it.
9. High Fidelity!!! 
10. John Williams is the man. Star Wars for starters then all the others. 

## 4. Movies that changed your perspective & stuck with you for months or years.

**Score:** 29
**Body:** I'm working on a list of films that changed me profoundly. Here's what I have so far. 

Children of Men 

I Saw the tv Glow 

A Thousand and One

The Night of the Hunter

Aftersun

Bone Tomahawk

The Father 

Flow

All that Breaths 

Men

Navalny

To Leslie  

Defending your Life  

The Sound of Metal 

Fish Tank  

Boogie Nights 

In the Bedroom  

Hoosiers 

The Master 

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. David Lynch’s Elephant Man will devastate you, but also make you think deeply about others. It is my all time favourite film for that reason.
  - I just learned that Mel Brooks produced it. The funny man, of all people.
2. Fight Club 
  - I love that movie. I saw a reel the other day where Ed Norton talked about how the film didn’t do well with audiences initially. Pitt and Norton snuck into the back of a test screening to see the film. The crowd reaction was horrible, and Norton looks at Pitt to see what his reaction was and Brad says to him, “This may be the best film I’ll ever be in”. I found that to be really profound. 
3. Parasite. I can't enjoy the rain anymore. Also the movie is a 10\10 must see. 
4. A clockwork orange- I got cured all right
5. Life Is Beautiful (1997) is a good one. I watched it when I was younger, but now that I’m a father, the movie just hits differently.
6. Ikiru

The film had a profound impact on my life. 
7. The Thin Red Line
8. The Road Warrior

## 5. Movies where I will say WTF every few minutes

**Score:** 24
**Body:** Hi friends, looking for some crazy (but good ) movies where you’d say WTF every few minutes.

I’m not only thinking about mindf\*ck movies but also weird stuff or visuals.

Death Stranding & Beetlejuice are first to come to mind, maybe even something like Everything, Everywhere all at once, The Science of Sleep, even the Like a Dragon game series.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. sorry to bother you. starts just kinda funny weird and then goes completely off the rails lol
  - His new one I Love Boosters is also great! 
2. SWISS ARMY MAN (2016)
3. Pretty much everything David Lynch made.
4. TETSUO: THE IRON MAN
5. not sure you'll say wtf to weird visuals, but definitely to drop dead gorgeous, borderline unreal visuals: The Fall (2006)
6. Inland Empire (2006)
7. John Dies at the End 

The first WTF comes about 30 seconds in, and the hits just keep coming after that. The screenplay was written by Don Coscarelli, who also directed. He’s also responsible for the Phantasm movies, so the man knows his WTF. Clancy Brown also plays a small role that’s good for about 5 WTFs all on its own. 
8. The Room(2003)
9. - Together (2025)
- Backrooms 
- Any Cronenberg movie
- The Substance is up there but mostly for the ending

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. The Odyssey

**Score:** 825
**Body:** Good morning / Good evening,

I watched the movie yesterday, and I genuinely think it has a strong chance of being the movie of the year.

I also believe there's a high chance it will win multiple Academy Awards. The stories featured in the film are well known and are rooted in ancient Greek history and mythology.

One thing that really surprised me was how incredibly loud the sound was. I honestly think it had the loudest audio I've ever experienced in a movie-even my smartwatch gave me a warning that the noise level was too high.

A few of the actors gave slightly weaker performances than I expected, but it didn't have much of an impact on the film as a whole.

I highly, highly recommend it.  
I enjoyed every minute of it.  
One thing I would strongly suggest for anyone planning to watch it in theaters is to book VIP seats (the one with the reclining seat) , as the movie is fairly long.

My rating is 8.7/10.

It's easily one of the top 15  
movies I've ever watch.

Thank you, and I hope everyone who watches it has a wonderful experience.

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Loud audio? Did Nolan get mad people couldn’t hear a word in Tenet? 
  - I remember Dunkirk being loud AF!
    - That first gunshot made everyone jump in the theater
2. “Top 15 films I’ve ever seen…”  
“8.7.”

Damn, you got high standards. 
  - Hey maybe they’ve only seen 30 total?
3. This post tells me that OP never saw Inception in theaters. Crazy sound mixing has become a Nolan staple
4. There is absolutely no excuse for Christopher Nolan to have poor audio engineering in yet another movie. He has had the budget of a small country to make his moves and audio is an issue every single time.
  - Yeah, it must be a choice at this point. 

The sound was amazing but I had trouble making out some dialogue.
5. I really enjoyed it but found some of the elements odd - occasionally the language because we're just used to seeing ancient epics in classical theatrical English. The pacing was funny, but so is the source material. 

The character and theme exploration was great. 

The score was amazing as expected. 

The dialogue was sometimes muffled... as expected. 

My wife thought it was historical so wasn't ready for the fantasy elements. 

Some parts were downright frightening.
6. https://preview.redd.it/v3c8v7wq4ldh1.jpeg?width=960&format=pjpg&auto=webp&s=c3b199d99e0f5ef471e897254a73d5d12c4e2ee4

## 2. The Odyssey (2026) Review - IMAX is Officially a Trojan Horse

**Score:** 754
**Body:** I went into this film with no pre-formed opinion. I didn't want to jump on the hate bandwagon before seeing it. Nolan has never made a truly bad movie ever. I've been entertained by almost everything he's made. So I went in open-minded.

Let me get the controversial stuff out of the way. The casting, modern English, modern accents, and historical inaccuracies didn't take me out of it one bit. I was completely fine with all of that.

My problem with this movie is it simply didn't have that "wow" factor.

Dinosaurs hit the big screen in 1993. There was nothing mind blowing about the cyclops or giants.

*Game of Thrones* and *House of the Dragon* offer more immersive and visually breathtaking visuals from the fantasy genre. *The Northman* had a far superior siege sequence than the siege of Troy, not to mention photography. On that note...

I was completely unimpressed by the photography. Worst of Nolan’s career. What’s the point of shooting large format if all you’re gonna do is shoot tight, shallow, crammed, darkly lit, claustrophobic, shoulder-mount camera shake shots?

The score was forgettable. So were the set pieces generally. And my god, the sound effects were bad. Every time I heard a sword stabbing or slitting someone, it just took me out. Pretty sure they just pulled sound effects from movies 20 years old. For a $250m movie, record some fresh fucking sound effects.

John Leguizamo and Samantha Morton gave the best performances in the movie.

There were some sequences I found quite engaging. I don't want to spoil it, but my favorite scene was mainly anchored by Elliot Page and had a truly absorbing atmosphere and tone to it.

The best element of the movie was the relationship between Damon and Himesh Patel. That should have been the primary focus of the story in my opinion.

I’m drawing a line in the sand right now. I have little further interest in movies shot in IMAX. I like large format, but I want a wide screen image. Would rather more movies just get shot in Alexa LF. I don't particularly love this movie, but *Roma* is a better example of what large format photography should look like.

You can’t have agile, high energy action sequences on IMAX like *Die Hard 3* \- taxi raging through Central Park. Everything has to be really stiff. If you give audiences *The Odyssey's* ocean sequences and have them watch back to back with *Master and Commander's* ocean sequences, I’d love to know what they think is more visually immersive and gripping. I'm willing to bet it's the latter.

**3/5.**

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Sooooo, honest question: 

How does it hold up to 1997's The Oddyssey with Armand Assante?
  - Homie asking the real questions 
    - Homer*
2. ![gif](giphy|BPKEhn9EvwxeWtwXVR)
  - Always the best
3. Did you watch it at a regular theater or an actual IMAX theater? We haven't been sure if we're going to see it because we can only see it at a normal theater and have seen those aspect ratio comparisons that make it look like the movie is always zoomed in.

Also, did the 3 hour length feel heavy or did the movie move briskly enough?
4. I thought the journey home was where Nolan did his best work in this film. I have issues elsewhere. When it comes down to it, I think this story was too big even for Nolan (or perhaps for a standalone film as a medium).
5. The best part of the movie for me was the third act, I also agree I wasn’t really blown away. Samantha Morton was the only part was i was interested, that and the underworld. A lot of the movie felt like that scene in POTC dead man’s chest when check is running away from natives on the beach 
6. Would the score have been the same if you went in with no expectations? also curious what are some of your favorite movies?
7. I just saw it myself. I saw it in IMAX.

I really enjoyed the movie. I need time to really digest it but I know my review is mostly positive. Some things were jaring at first, such as the American accents but I settled into them after a little bit. John Bernthal's performance really made me stop caring about the accents. 

One thing that really felt odd was the story itself but this is now about the actual story of the Odyssey. It has the feel of an old story in that it's kind of nonsense. Similar to the feeling I get when I hear OG Knights of the Round Table stories. They are not very compatible with how we tell stories in the modern era. 

The performances were pretty great across the board. I felt emersed by the visuals. Overall, it was great. I can confidently say this is my favorite version of the Odyssey. 

## 3. Which movie character stole the show as the hilarious best friend? I'd go with Wallace Wells from Scott Pilgrim vs. the World.

**Score:** 565

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. John Goodman in The Big Lebowski  
![gif](giphy|tZ4QzCueTwh2g)
  - Calmer than you are.
2. ![gif](giphy|3o7abwwXjpaWDBRu9i)


3. Michael Peña as Luis in “Ant-Man”

https://preview.redd.it/77f0n8xq9ldh1.jpeg?width=640&format=pjpg&auto=webp&s=028cee608083e1cf4d399720adffef06d0f8c812
4. ![gif](giphy|vPIno8azLyVT3oZwH1)
5. https://i.redd.it/dnouir1ebkdh1.gif
6. ![gif](giphy|L44n0n5YLyyNC5SC9J)
7. Chunk (played by Lawrence Cohen) in The Goonies (1985)


8. Didn't just steal the show... Stole Stacey Pilgrim's boyfriend... Multiple times
9. ![gif](giphy|fqISmyAPIRsfS)

OG Bestie with a pint at the Winchester. How's that for a slice of fried gold?

## 4. What’s your favorite vampire movie?

**Score:** 236
**Body:** Lost Boys (1987) for me.

Its stylish take on the vampire trope and blending of horror and comedy works so well. Also has one of my favorite Kiefer Sutherland performance.

What’s your favorite vampire movie?

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. ![gif](giphy|3oEdvc84IW7P3qbQFq)
  - https://i.redd.it/10zosbm25odh1.gif
2. https://preview.redd.it/ptliueg06odh1.png?width=459&format=png&auto=webp&s=ac090284ec9196f79e7d49c4139983914b771953


3. ![gif](giphy|qf7LdVZXgsiKk)
4. What we do in the shadows.

![gif](giphy|8DyxgHqSyeGTm)
5. 30 Days of Night. I loved the more animalistic take on vampires than we usually get, with them still being intelligent and even having their own language.
6. Near Dark
7. Fright Night
8. Only Lovers Left Alive 
9. Let the Right One in

## 5. Corey Feldman turns 55 years old today. What are some of your favorite movies he’s been in?

**Score:** 186

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Lost Boys..
  - ![gif](giphy|CeaEuiQ7RY59K)
2. Stand by Me, The Burbs
  - *Hey, Mrs. Rumsfield, no tan lines!*
    - https://i.redd.it/qaolt5jkzldh1.gif
3. License to Drive is a movie that comes to mind when I hear his name. 
  - Ah…the movie that introduced me to Heather Graham. 
4. I’ll start. The Goonies 
  - The answer is ALWAYS The Goonies 💯
5. The Burbs

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. Thoughts on The Odyssey

**Score:** 149
**Body:** &#x200B;

Just got out and of the theatre and will use this post to collect my thoughts a bit. Will keep it as spoiler free as possible.

Overall the movie is pretty good, a solid 8/10 and probably the best movie I've seen this year. 

Some sequences were excellent. The Cyclops sequence is maybe the best thing Nolan has ever done. The scenes  in Hades and with Circe were also amazing, as was the Trojan horse story that Menelaus tells. 

On the other hand, the scenes with Calypso left a lot to be desired and felt rushed. The movie probably could have done with another 15-20 minutes to explore the relationship between Odysseus and Calypso and given Charlize Theron a bit more to work with. The final action sequence is also kind of...bad. It felt a bit jarring and out of place when the rest of the action in the movie up to that point and been well done and kept fairly grounded. 

The editing in some of the scenes also fell a bit short. Some scenes weren't allowed to breathe with to many cuts and the jumping back and forth. 

The modernised dialogue was a bit jarring at times but it generally works and is not as bad as the trailer made it seem.

The acting was generally pretty solid, Elliott Page, Robert Pattinson and Himesh Patel being the standouts. Matt Damon wasn't the perfect casting, he's just too much of a nice guy to get across how cunning and menacing the character of Odysseus is; Sean Bean is Troy, by comparison, gets that across pretty well. However, in more intimate scenes Matt Damon does well.

The big issue with the movie is the horrible miscasting of Tom Holland who simply fails to hold his own against the actors sharing the screen with him. Many scenes have other actors (like Hathaway, Pattinson, Nyong'o) chewing the scenery and really getting into the dialogue while Holland just seems like he's reading lines. 

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. "My Dad is coming home" said with the same emotional depth as "I want fruit for breakfast today."

Holland is always bland. He was miscast in the role. 

Some people think he should be the new Bond and I can only imagine him in that role.

"I'm going to shoot you now unless you tell me the information that I need" said with the same emotional depth as "I want oat milk in my latte today."

I've seen a few posts from people who thought the final third of the movie was the best part so I'm surprised you didn't think the final battle was good unless it's not in that part.
  - > Some people think he should be the new Bond

That's insane.  He looks 16 and is about 5'6.  Perfect for Spiderman.  Horrible for Bond, just based on appearance, nevermind acting ability.
  - Tom Holland is not a very good actor. He's good for Spiderman but it's really not a very demanding role. Everything else he has done has been mediocre or just bad.
  - The final third of the movie was good until the final battle which feels like a massive shift in tone
    - Maybe I shouldn't be commenting about this without having seen the movie, but this doesn't shock me at all to hear, but I wonder if it was actually a problem with the end of the movie or the lead up to it. 

I find a pretty common complaint I hear about Nolan's movie is that it's all really great, but then at some point in the third act it loses momentum and starts to feel overly long, but as the saying goes: third act problems are first act problems.  If your movie is stumbling in the third act it's most likely a problem with the set up, rather than the third act itself. Nolan tends to pace every part of his films like it's the climax of an action movie. The camera is often moving quickly, there is a cut every 1-2 seconds, driving music scores every scene, there is no beat between scenes, dialogue tends to be either very expository or character directly stating themes at the camera, and even establishing shots tend to be big swirling overhead shots where loud music blasts at you. By the time you get to the third act there isn't another gear to into to up the stakes, you just keep going at the same pace as before. Additionally, directing action is one of the the things Nolan is weakest at directing, so if the film climaxes in a big action set piece, as his films often do, it feels lackluster, especially after you've already being going at a breakneck speed for the previous two hours. I find that the way he paces his films also tends to paper over a lot of the sizeable cracks in his writing, so that in the moment you don't notice that the writing is often quite weak.
2. Yeah also hated the final action sequence felt very out of place too long and cliché choreography. Lots of scenes lack impact because the editing is way to fast very bad rythm overall imo. And the design of scylla is very bad. 

It’s made imo too much like an action movie and doesn’t let us feel emotions or even the epic scale of the story
  - Lots of scenes lack impact because the editing is way to fast very bad rythm overall imo

This is honestly probably my biggest criticism with Christopher Nolan in general. I don't remember how Memento played out anymore (lol) but every subsequent movie by him shares this similar kind of rapid-paced editing and sequencing of time & events that I find particularly jarring.
3. Where would you rank it with other Nolan movies you saw? Top 3, top 5, or top 10

Yeah, Damon is my biggest concern and has been since I saw the trailer. I am big on casting and acting with any movie and I just do not buy him as Odysseus. That is the one thing that irks me before I watch it, not sure if I'll believe it with him as the lead. I think they needed someone who acts with more depth and emotion, He has very limited range as an actor. I would have cast Russel Crowe, even older and fatter lol, or even Gerard Butler before Damon
4. You nailed my exact thoughts. I whispered the comment to my friend about the editing. I felt they were cutting near immediately after each moment that shouldve been felt. The final fight scene felt like generic action with little creative choices, just cliche hero hacking down useless nobodies. I did feel a little bit bored at times but my attention span is pretty weak.


But, that Trojan Horse scene..... oh my god 10/10. The slow build of the score and tension, the dragging out of tension for sooo long. From the beach to the gate. The torch flaring and revealing the army was aweeessoommmeee. Finally when those gates dropped was awesome in cinema. At home I'd probably think it was corny but in theatre I FELT the deafening drumming in my chest until the roars of battle came.

My main issue is, the main cast is so recognisable it almost hurts the film. I just see Jason Bourne, Spiderman, Edward Cullen, The Punisher, furiosa Juno etc and I like all these people but I just couldn't hold my immersion.

Overall yeah, 8.5/10
  - > I felt they were cutting near immediately after each moment that shouldve been felt. 

It's Nolan.  What were you expecting?  Gotta keep up the illusion that you're watching something super important happen.  Can't give you time to realize the movie actually *rest of sentence drowned out by booming Hans Zimmer score*

## 2. Why do non-IMAX theaters crop the IMAX frame instead of just projecting it smaller? Contractual restrictions or creative choices?

**Score:** 33
**Body:** A friend brought this up and now I'm curious.

Why do non-IMAX theaters crop the IMAX frame instead of just projecting the full image slightly smaller, leaving black bars or unused screen space on the sides. I mean, I'm not talking about the film print itself. With a digital DCP, couldn't you just project the full image at a smaller size instead of cropping it? You'd preserve the entire frame.

He mentioned that there are contractual restrictions when shooting w IMAX cameras that only allow the full frame to be shown in actual IMAX theaters. Is that actually true, or is it just a projection / distribution choice?

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. That’s not what’s happening at all. The film was shot with both IMAX and widescreen formats in mind. A better way to think about it is that it was shot for widescreen, but with extra space at the top and bottom specifically for IMAX presentation. 

When shooting for IMAX, you always want to leave mostly empty space at the top and bottom and put all the action in the center of the frame. This is because the size of the screen means people would be moving their heads and craning their necks to see everything. 

If you want to see a film shot in a narrow aspect ratio that IS supposed to be viewed that way in all formats, look at Bugonia. It was shot in 1.5:1 VistaVision and the entire frame is filled with action. That’s not how Nolan shot The Odyssey. He didn’t fill the entire frame because that’s not how you shoot for IMAX. 
  - what a really well thought out response! this totally made me understand why they dont just do that
2. I'm fairly sure they don't. Regular cinemas dont receive 'IMAX' DCPs, they receive P3 DCI 2K or 4K DCPs in 1.85:1 or 2.39:1. 

It's kinda nice how everyone seems so interested in this topic all of the sudden, because of The Odyssey releasing I guess.
  - Exactly this. The theatres aren’t cropping anything, they’re projecting the film as it is supplied to them.

There is nothing stopping Nolan or anyone else from creating the DCP with the IMAX aspect ratio intact (and with pillarboxing on the sides). They choose not to, presumably concluding that horizontal resolution is preferable.

Ultimately a ‘4K’ DCP that keeps the 1.43:1 IMAX ratio needs to be shrunk down so much to fit into the 1.85:1 DCP ratio that it technically isn’t anywhere near 4K anyway - visible picture area would be 3088x2160 which would almost certainly lead to complaints from audiences.
3. Sometimes linking to Twitter is iffy in Reddit and sometimes bans the message, so I’ll just copy/paste what someone on Twitter said about this. He’s @abdalhadealani on Twitter:

“Hey, I’m a cinematographer and technician with 10 years of experience in the industry and education. I think I can break this down for you in a pretty straightforward way:

IMAX isn't just about a different aspect ratio, it's an entirely different projection system. Those IMAX film reels are massive, carrying significantly more resolution and optical data than standard film. Because of this, they require specialized projection hardware to handle all that fidelity that the production company and the director intended to capture. If you try to run IMAX footage through standard cinema projectors, you're going to lose a ton of resolution, sharpness, and detail, not to mention that IMAX is engineered specifically for massive, immersive screens, not your typical multiplex setup.

Also, standard theaters use screens and projectors calibrated for a 2.39:1 aspect ratio. Forcing a 4:3 image onto that setup requires extra processing, which often leads to a noticeable drop in image brightness and overall quality compared to the native IMAX experience.

Most importantly, if you project an IMAX film in 4:3 on a screen built for 16:9 or 18:9, you’re going to end up with huge pillarboxing (black bars on the sides). This makes the image look tiny, which kills the immersion and ruins the intended cinematic experience. Audience immersion is everything, and every technical choice is made to pull you deeper into the frame. That’s why you can’t just play IMAX footage in a standard theater without cropping the hell out of the image to make it "fit."

There’s a lot more to it, but I think these three points cover the core of the issue without getting too deep into the weeds.”
4. As a viewer I would vastly prefer the "correct" aspect ratio even if it meant the whole screen wasn't filled up. We're living the freakin' DVD fullscreen vs widescreen wars all over again but backwards lol  


repeat: As a viewer I would vastly prefer the "correct" aspect ratio even if it meant the "whole screen" wasn't filled up. We're living the freakin' DVD fullscreen vs widescreen wars all over again but backwards lol
  - The thing is, when a 70mm IMAX image is 1.43:1 aspect, it’s not just “a more square picture”. When you frame things for IMAX projection, you’re assuming it will be on a 75-100 foot tall screen. The audience will be concentrating their view on a point around 1/3 the way up. That’s where you need to place your main action, your close up faces, etc. The top and far edges of the frame fall away to the audiences’ peripheral vision.

If you just project an IMAX image onto a normal widescreen, not only would the image be much smaller, only taking up the very center of the screen, but it would look poorly framed, like everything was shot from farther away than you’d expect, with way too much headroom.
    - Exactly this. 
  - Except that in this case, there is no “correct” aspect ratio.
  - there *is* no “correct” AR, they intentionally made specifics ARs for specific types of screens. If you’re watching it in IMAX, they’ll project the correct version. If you’re watching it on a widescreen theater display, they’ll project the correct version, too.

## 3. 'Lone Star' (1996), Dir. John Sayles

**Score:** 5
**Body:** ***"Yeah, it's always heartwarming to see a prejudice defeated by a deeper prejudice."***

John Sayles's 'Lone Star' is my first foray into his filmography. I was very warmly invited, even urged, to watch this film at once when I came across its seemingly American pastoral aesthetic, the use of Chris Cooper in a leading role, a young Matthew McConaughey, and the promise of a 'neo-western' narrative. What I watched was one of the closest cinematic equivalents to the density of a novel that I have seen. 

Soon into its runtime, my impressions of the filmmaking centred on the muscularity of Sayles's direction – smooth, heady transitions into flashbacks told with serious conviction; rich dialogue that lets these characters meet us fully-formed as people in the small and fictional South Texan town of Frontera; and an intermingling cast of characters, all of whose lives are established and told with measured beginnings, middles, and ends to their personal dilemmas and concerns. 

Floating above this medley of Texans is the cloud that concerns their coexistence: the teetering harmony between the Native American, Latino, Black, and White European populations that inhabit it and the people, like Elizabeth Peña's teacher Pilar, who are trying with all their might to keep everyone glued together. The town Sayles devised for the film translates to 'border' in Spanish, and these people inhabit it with a friction bound to be beckoned by its location and name. 

Regrettably, as an English observer, the state of American politics and racial tensions often seems and feels worse today than what I saw unfold on-screen. Thirty years since the release of the film, tensions at and within the borders of the US still rage and periodically burn the country down. In fact, US leadership has and continues to frequently encroach borders it ought to have avoided, but that's a conversation of war crimes and human rights infractions for another day. 

'History is written by the victors' is the chief consideration of Sayles's screenplay. In a town as simultaneously coalesced and divided as Frontera, each ethnic group holds and recites its distinct version of events: the outcomes of interethnic wars, the colonisation of the US, and even the omissions that should be made to avoid damaging the purity of broad ethnic reputations. We see Pilar leading a parents' conference where Latino parents argue with White parents, White parents argue with Black parents, and they all pontificate on the way history ought to be taught at the school; it's a microcosm of the larger disagreements that riddle the US with resentment at all levels of societal organisation, picking and choosing convenient details. The crux of it is often that every other group believes White Europeans received the final word on what history's path was, leaving everybody else with a version that belies the harsher realities of their lives: the losses of land, dignity, and autonomy they endured as America was forged. 

In the first few minutes, when the pivot upon which the film's story rests, the discovery of a skeleton dressed in nought but a sheriff's badge and Masonic ring, is introduced, one gets the feeling that the narrative will be at least somewhat treading 'thriller' territory, with Cooper's sheriff, Sam Deeds, leading the charge; quite the contrary happens. As mentioned before, 'Lone Star's conceit very much focuses on the inner lives of its town's inhabitants, stripped of any cagey or nail-biter scenes. Sayles sought to investigate the bugbears that threaten the cohesion of these disparate communities whilst equally considering the problems that are bringing individual lives to a head. 

One such problem is the return of Joe Morton's Delmore Payne to the town. Desmond is a US Army colonel who has been posted at Frontera for three years, his issue being that the father who abandoned him, Otis, resides there. Otis is a pillar of the Black community, running the only casual establishment, a nightclub, that is a safe haven for local Black folks. Delmore overcompensates for the lack of paternal authority in his childhood by looming large over his own son, and we can envision the rotten fruits that dynamic could produce if Desmond persists in his ways. Otis also recognises the damage he has caused his son and, in turn, his grandson, and slowly an indirect agreement forms between Delmore and Otis to return what was lost between them and ensure they can coexist for the good of the next generation. A didactic message from Sayles to the rest of the country, perhaps.

A particularly piquant thread woven into this tapestry of Texans is Pilar's mother, Mercedes, who was an illegal immigrant. Mercedes is one of those puzzlingly conservative immigrants who call border patrol on people entering a country illegally, like she did, whilst also hiring undocumented Mexicans at her restaurant. This remains a pertinent hypocrisy given the contingent of Latin Americans who "got theirs" in the US and who believe that by some miraculous idiosyncrasy of their backstory, their fellow countrymen and immigrants aren't entitled to the same empathy and admittance. Sayles's portrayal of Mercedes struck me as surprisingly candid and vulnerable; we can watch the guilt of her deep-set conscience dawn on her each time she enacts some act of betrayal or superiority, insecurities bubbling to the surface through her projections and tattling, and it is painful to see the affectations she so clearly knows she is putting on to seem more American than her fellow Mexican-Americans.

The looming ghost of the story is, of course, the damned soul of that skeleton – the former sheriff, Charlie Wade. Wade is infamous across town for his iniquity, his unlawful corruptions and trigger-happy responses to perceived slights and threats; he's a character who wouldn't be out of place in Jorge Luis Borges' short story collection of disreputable rapscallions, 'A Universal History of Infamy'. Sam Deeds is told by Mayor Hollis Pogue, a retired deputy sheriff, that his father, McConaughey's flashback feature as Buddy Deeds, had publicly argued with Wade the night before his disappearance. Wade was presumed to have skipped town with a bagful of money, but the bag of bones poses the matter of his murder and who by. 

Sam's personal problem is his disgruntlement – with his love life, with the residents in town, and most of all, with his tyrant father Buddy's shortcomings – so he hopes to find that his father, a mythic hero of the town in the vein of Ransom Stoddard from 'The Man Who Shot Liberty Valance', was the murderer. All Sam receives are anecdotes-turned-paeans glorifying Buddy even higher, and McConaughey was elastic and striking enough in his short screen time to contain these multitudes in his performance. The narrative ends up fitting a rough mould of 'Oedipus Rex' in so far as the exhumation of shocking fates is concerned.

Bar none, the characters in this story must reckon with the truths that other people hold and the contradictions that these cause for their own worldviews and histories. To Sam, his father was a prescriptive, overrated lawman inside and outside the home, a man who prevented the fulfilment of his teenage love, Pilar. To the rest of the town, Sam was the embodiment of justice, the other side of the coin that held Charlie Wade's ghastly glower in profile. Sam comes to learn that the objective truths occupy a space somewhere in the middle, half-rhymes ringing across town as every other character finds similar shades of grey. 

On the subject of love, Pilar and Sam's relationship is revisited. It's the second skeleton of the film; we just don't see any skulls peering back at us. The couple were in love in high school and haven't seen one another in twenty-three years, Sam recently divorced and Pilar having had two children in the meantime. Their chosen love juxtaposes all of the familial relationships brought into small degrees of jeopardy and questioning, but even their romance has its challenges, which we later discover. The fondness they retained for one another is very touching to watch amid the mosaic of love lacking everywhere else, an anchor for us to hold onto like hope for a country's future.

So the interstices of those overarching reflections on America are filled by the individualistic idea that we write our personal histories, and sometimes the immediacy of our own lives causes more strife for us than the banes of the country's. Sayles made certain that these characters unravel as they question and rewrite the stories and facts that constitute their histories so far. What we find in the end is that there is genuine, gritty work to be done before we can resolve any conclusions about other people in our lives, and the same must be said of those who are near our lives – the people that we coexist with, regardless of their creed or colour – there is a requisite effort that will keep America churning if that effort is only discharged.

John Sayles: So far, so good. He has been a wonderful revelation, and I can only imagine how much I will enjoy the rest of his body of work. Chris Cooper leads this film with perfect poise and a manner of settling into his character that I scarcely see elsewhere; if Buddy Deeds is a man of myths, the stuff of legends, then Sam Deeds is made real, as authentic as can be, by Chris Cooper's adept personification. Around the scenes of 'Lone Star' are the calming Southern landscapes and nostalgically lit rooms of small-town Texas; within those rooms, we see how the wounds of American history scarred differently.

## 4. A tale of 2 celebrities (The Valet and Borderline)

**Score:** 1
**Body:** So recently ive been getting into the work of Samara Weaving and I must say shes really fantastic. If only she was in better movies. However thst doesnt stop her from giving it her all. As I continue forward into her filmography I see someone whos able to blend in and not play herself. She looks completely different in a lot her projects. Even though theres characters look similar I can tell apart Olivia Allen from The Valet and Nix in Gun akimbo. 

Today I want to talk about Olivia Allen and Sofia Minor from the movie borderline and how they each parallel each other. Lets start off with Sofia minor from Borderline although a big celebrity shes very down to earth as you can tell by watching that one dance sequence. This whole sequence is acted phenomenally by Weaving as she gives the character the much needed gravitas and warmth that Jimmy Warden forgot to give her. No offense to him, but he didnt really flesh out any of these characters enough. So thats where his wife comes in and once again shes fantastic. You don't see samara weaving, you see Sofia Minor and she just acts naturally. You cna tell that sofia has connections and other friends which isnt surprising for a celebrity, but something about her feels so relatable and down to earth. She maybe on every billboard in town, bur shes not your average pop star. Unfortunately we arent really given a lot of Sofia outside life. Instead we see her being intruded by a maniac. So she doesnt really change much. 

Now compare her to Olivia Allen at the beginning of The Valet, who starts off very detached from the world and in a relationship with a criminal politician no less, but as the movie progresses, She meets a valet who will change her life and that is what leads to her becoming less over the top and more like a real person. The over the top pretentious personality makes it feel as if Olivias just acting out her life  instead of being a real person. Its not until she meets Antonio, a civilian whom humbles her into someone better. 

So I think these 2 roles pretty much showcase how brilliant of an actress Samara is and how much energy she brings to the table. She disappears into every single role she plays and I dont think of her as samara instead I think of them as these characters which she brought to life. Eenie meanie would've been even more boring if it weren't for her performance. Overall, I love how shes her own beast instead of being "Uncle Hugo's offering to hollywood".

## 5. Minions and Monsters: Is it all about the intent?

**Body:** I remember reading a book about magics and the occult. The author talked about how vocalization isn't always necessary for spells and rituals, but it doesn't mean that vocalization is useless in those cases; that it could help the caster or dancer in other ways.

This crossed my mind when I watched Minions and Monsters today. It's shown that Minions can't read, or they can read, but the language is their own even if the actual diction and text and alphabet is the same.

In turn, this made me wonder how they're able to use the magic. The way they say what they read won't be the same as the humans of that period. This must mean that the magic spells work in a way that doesn't actually require the exact sounds/words that humans used when casting. The closest thing I can come up with is intention. Maybe the magics worked by knowing a person knowing what they want and asking for it in the way that counts for them. Does anyone have any other ideas?
